Here's the deal. One thing I really haven't talked about that much yet, and I'm going to really start focusing on as we start growing together. Remember, this is a long-term program. It's ongoing, continuing education. It's meant to just make you better and better and better and get sharper forever basically. There's no beginning or end here. It's about continued and never-ending improvement. So here's the deal. One thing we haven't spoken about is how do you develop a customer? What's the best time not just to get referrals from a customer? I spoke about that once and I'll repeat that in a while. But how do you take a customer who's bought from you once and turn them into a real raving fan? How do you essentially in the brokerage industry recall that? I'm going to sneeze at this fucking fire or something. There's shit in the air. Oh my God. It's coming. Anyway, how do you take someone in the brokerage industry call that second trade them? In other words, someone would come in and you'd sell them $5,000 worth of a stock. And then you wouldn't really make any money on that, but you would show them what you could do for them. And hopefully you'd make the money and you'd call them back a week later after their check-in and after they saw that you were real. And it really didn't matter what was going on with the stock. Obviously, you want it to be up. But the point was just by them becoming a customer and seeing that you're for real, that they sent their money in, that the stock showed up in their account, that it was legitimate, right? People are smart. They get that. Okay. So now they're willing to spend essentially or invest more money because they wanted a certain outcome in the first place. They were just dabbling to start. So that's true pretty much in every industry you're in. Now, sometimes, let's say if you're a car salesperson, it's not even going to sell someone a car. Then next week you're selling another car. That's probably not going to happen unless you're selling for someone else in the family. So that's a little bit different. So it's really more about getting referrals, building your business by maybe helping other people in the family. But it's really not like a repeat where every few weeks you're going to get another big sale out of them because it's just not like that. But in other industries, it is like that. In the information industry, when you're selling programs even like mine or if you're in insurance where you start off on a smaller program and there's other types of insurance you could sell them, or in your network marketing industry where someone starts off and they're just dabbling with something and they're at a low level and they decide to upgrade. In other words, once you open up an account with them, they become a customer, that's when the relationship really has a chance to begin. That's when you can really start to grow it. Don't look at that as an endpoint. That's a beginning point because the bottom line is that if someone bores from you once, you're job, you're obligation to make sure they have a great experience. You want to, again, this goes back somewhat to creating customers for life, but in the context of let's monetize it. In other words, it starts with this one overarching concept. You must under promise and over deliver. That's how it starts. Like for instance, with this program I created here, I started off with weekly intensives and I promised X and I delivered X times 10. I added things on. I created a much more robust backend. I do a little extra Q and A's. I put bonus trains. I'm always doing things and I work harder at it to make sure that every single person who signs up for this knows that not only am I giving them the value they expected, but they're getting more value. I want them to understand that's a philosophy of how I operate. Everything they do with me, every product I ever offer, they understand that. Whether it fits my needs or maybe it doesn't. But the point is I know one thing that this guy has got a track record of over delivering on his promises. That's key. That's where the whole process starts. Make sure that you manage expectation effectively. Don't promise crazy shit. Don't duck phone calls. It goes back again to customers for life. But now let's talk about the monetization. What do you do now? You sold something, right? You promised them why? You're living why and then some and they're happy. They're thrilled. Well, number one, you have no way of selling them something else or giving them more value unless you call them or write to them. So you got to keep in touch with your people. I want you to look at this as a program. I always think in terms of programs. I would never just go about willy nilly trying to sell someone something. Like, okay, let's say I have a certification program, right? It's an incredibly robust program for salespeople. They get certified. They go through training. They get tested. It's a much different thing, right? But the point is I would never just launch that like, oh, I'm interested to see if someone wants to buy that, right? If I'm going to do that, I'm going to actually organize it and do it. Ask every single person in a regimented way the same day in the sales cycle. In other words, you know, they start off, they spend X number of days in this program. I want to know on what day is the best to call them. Think about your business. Now, everyone has a different business, right? Whatever your business is, I want you to map this out today. What is the best day in the cycle? So you close the sale on January 1 on Monday. Let's say that check, they pay for it three days later. So the check hits three days. What's the best day to approach them with another product to sell them something else? And remember the key here is you don't want to be doing this just to make money. That's not a reason to sell something. That is not sustainable. You can make a little money over the short term, but it's not going to end well. What I'm talking about here is something very different. What I'm talking about is if you have products that are really going to benefit your customers, that they can really need, that they really need, they can really use and that will resolve some pain point. That's what I'm talking about. So if you're doing this in a good way, maybe you don't have that right now, but I want you to find it. Go out and get it. Take a good, hard look at what you're doing. If you don't have something right now to sell the people you've just sold to, then you're making a big mistake because you're doing them a favor. Someone just bought from you. Let's flip the switch here. Look at the other way. Watch, right? Someone just bought it from you. You over deliver. You gave them a great product that thrilled the sale. They love you. They're like, I made a great decision, right? Well, don't you think that person would want to know if you had something of great value to them that would, like if they invested $5, they'd get back 50 or 10 or whatever. Don't you think they want, you're obligated to tell them? See the problem is you stop thinking like they're selling. You're giving value to people. You're a provider of value. And yes, as sales people, we get paid handsomely for that because nothing happens in this world until someone sells something. But it starts with you number one, recognizing that this is nothing evil or manipulative. This is how business operates. Every great Amazon, one of the best companies out there, right? Apple, they always think in terms of a host, a community of products, a series of products, getting people into the communities that they buy. One thing they buy, 10 things. With Apple, their entire resurrection was based on what? The iPod. Little freaking music player. That's how it all started. And once people got that, and the iPhone, of course, became sort of a host suite of products, right? And they make the pain of disconnection so high because you love the product so much, then you buy everything Apple. Amazon the same thing. Or they started selling you books like, wait a second, what else can we sell? Not because we want to rip them off or be, aha, we got them with books and now we're going to get them with everything. People want to be sold to, or let me put it, people love to buy. They hate to be sold to. So you obviously don't go out there like some hard-charging salesperson. You're a provider of value, of convenience. So if I have something that I know is going to be of great value to you, shame on me. If I don't make it easy, I don't make it affordable, and I don't put it in front of you and let you at least know about it. Now this is very different than the disingenuousness that you see there that really freaked me out and started this whole, this program I have with free webinars. That's disingenuous bull. Well they'll sit there and they'll waste a couple of hours of your time pretending to teach you something, but not teaching you anything. And then they slowly transition to sell you at the end, and they beat you to get you wet the whole time by telling you how great this is. They don't teach you anything, right? That's not what I mean. That is disingenuous. It's bull. And that's why my business is flourishing now, because I said, no, I'm not doing that. If I'm going to train people, I'm going to train them. You can't train people and sell to them at the same time. You get it? So notice I don't, that's, it gives sales a bad name. I'm talking about something very different. I'm talking about looking at your customer, knowing who your customer is and providing them with what they need in a very easy, cost-effective way. And bam, you're going to make a ton of money. That's what I want you to be thinking about today. So I want you to look at it as a problem with every single person that comes into your purview. They go through your funnel, you close them. What's next? What's your next step? First of all, you better call them within the first seven days. I'll make sure they receive at least something from you, a training, an email, something, a thank you note, whatever it might be. Get in touch with them. Depending on the industry, you know, some things make sense and some don't. Right? But the point is, is that after their check hits, after they've paid actually, you don't want to wait more than a few days. Seriously, don't wait more than a few days. They don't have to, in the same way that you can get referrals from someone, as soon as they say yes and they buy, the same thing goes with a second product. Although you want to let them at least experience what you're selling them. In other words, if someone closes something with me, I'm not going to try to sell something else a few days. I'll give them a week or two. Let them at least see how great it is. And the point is, if you have confidence in your product and you're better, brother, if not, then sell something else. Okay? Like, I know, listen, I know that if I put someone on this program, they're going to love it. I've priced it that way and I've given, you know, I've done so much, you know, put my own money and time into this to make it awesome. So people, I know when I say, hey, what do you think? Oh my God, it's great. That sets up a long-term relationship and shame on you if you're not actually capitalizing on that, not officially, it's up to them too. People have needs that must get filled. It's your job as a salesperson to fill that need. That's what you do. Stop thinking like you're a salesman, it's some shifty thing. It's not like that. As long as you're providing them with great products at a great price and over-delivering on the value, you do people a favor. Every person that you close must go through some sort of an ascension mouth and turn them into long-term customers. You know why? Because if you don't, someone else will do it and they might not be as ethical as you. They might not care about giving value as much as you do. You owe it to your customers, you owe it to yourself, your own pocket, by the way. It's one of those situations where all the forces align. You get it? Everyone's in the same boat here. We're all on the same page. It's win-win. That's what I call a fricking business model that makes sense and is sustainable. That's why I've always been so, you know, in bed actually guys, you've got to give value that are over-delivered. I don't just mean it as a punchline. Seriously, it's not a punchline. It's the truth. It's how I build my business and how I want you to build yours and watch what happens. Now, obviously, there's work to be done. You've got to create the value. You've got to package it the right way. You've got to make it easy. And also, you've got to become a master closer because you have to be able to tell the story. You have to be able to position it in a way that it's sexy as hell, that the benefits are apparent and because they already trust you. Remember those three tens. You've already established the second ten. They trust you. You've sold them something. You've made some promises and guess what? You've over-delivered your promises. You gave them an ensign. How easy is it to sell them something else provided they need it, they want it. There's a pain that you can help relieve and that you continue this process of over-delivering. That's how you build a long-term business and get rich. So think in terms of programs. I want you to write it down. How many days are going to lapse before I reach out to my new customer? How many days until I try to sell them something? What am I going to sell them? What are the benefits? Make sure that you price it in a way that you're giving more value than you're asking for in money. That's the key. Wrap your brain, come up with add-ons and bonuses and stacks. You want to do the right thing by your customer. This is not a get rich quicksum. This is long-term wealth creation. Having the same people buy for you again and again and again because they love you, they trust you, you give them awesome stuff. That's the bottom line. So go out there today and kick ass, but remember it's not just about always cold calling the new people. Go back to the old ones. Start developing a long-term repeat business and if you can put them on continuity where they keep paying you every month, price your programs lower. You don't make as much of them as I did. I'm losing money short-term, no doubt, right? But I said, you know, if I believe in what I have and I know it's awesome, then I will make it up over the long term because people will stay on it. Don't be scared to do that. You don't get rich quickly, but you give massive value. People are happy and then over the long, you make even more. That's the ticket. All right. I love you all. Get your ass out there today and bang on as many doors as you have to, as many calls you have to, but don't neglect the people you have. Want you to focus today, turn it into a program. It's an actual system of how you go about escalating any person who comes into your funnel and then buys. How do they become a long-term customer? Because again and again and again, think Amazon, think Apple, think about yourself in your own pocket if you do that right. And make sure you always give more value than you ask for in money. And just watch what happens. You'll get very rich. Love you all. Talk to you tomorrow.
